1:
"When Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father in law, heard of all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel his people, and that the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt;" The witnesses note Jethro hearing of God's deliverance of Israel. On "Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father in law, heard of all that God had done," the grammatical voices note the report. Henry and Gill note that "when Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' father in law, heard of all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel" — Jethro (Reuel, Exodus 2:18; the priest or prince of Midian, Moses' father-in-law) heard of the great deliverance (the plagues, the Red Sea, the wonders); the report spread abroad (the fame of God's works, Exodus 15:14; Joshua 2:10); the occasion of his visit.

On "all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel his people," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the deliverance. Calvin and Ellicott note the things God had done (for Moses and Israel) — the great works of God; the fame of the deliverance reaching Midian; the report that drew Jethro.

On "the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the Exodus: "the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt" — the great deliverance (the Exodus, the chief of God's works); the report that moved Jethro to come (vv.5); the providence (the fame of God's redemption drawing the Gentile priest). The verse displays Jethro hearing of God's deliverance of Israel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the report — "Jethro... heard of all that God had done... the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt," the fame (Exodus 15:14, Henry, Gill); "all that God had done for Moses, and for Israel his people," the deliverance (Calvin, Ellicott); "the LORD had brought Israel out of Egypt," the Exodus (vv.5, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hearing Jethro. The verse marks the Jethro: father-in-law. The verse displays the report: God's works. The verse displays the Exodus: heard. The verse displays the visit: occasioned. The report displays the fame of God's deliverance. The verse displays the Jethro: hearing. The verse displays the deliverance: famed. The verse displays the visit: to come. Jethro hearing of God's deliverance of Israel displays the fame of the redemption: Jethro heard all God had done, the LORD bringing Israel out of Egypt (Exodus 15:14; Joshua 2:10) — the great deliverance drawing the Gentile priest to come. The verse displays Jethro bringing Moses' family (vv.2-6).
2:
"Then Jethro, Moses' father in law, took Zipporah, Moses' wife, after he had sent her back," The very brief verse notes Jethro bringing Zipporah, Moses' wife. The grammatical voices note the family. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Jethro, Moses' father in law, took Zipporah, Moses' wife, after he had sent her back" — Jethro brought Zipporah (Moses' wife) and her sons (vv.3-4) to Moses; Zipporah having been "sent back" earlier (perhaps after the incident of the circumcision, Exodus 4:24-26, or sent home for safety during the Egyptian conflict); the reunion of Moses' family; the bringing of the wife and sons.

On "took Zipporah, Moses' wife," the witnesses note the wife. Gill notes Zipporah (Moses' wife, Exodus 2:21) — brought by her father to Moses; the reunion.

On "after he had sent her back," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the separation: "after he had sent her back" — Zipporah sent home earlier (to Midian, for safety or other cause, Exodus 4:24-26); now restored to Moses; the providence (the family reunited at the mount of God, vv.5). The verse displays Jethro bringing Zipporah, Moses' wife.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the family — "Jethro... took Zipporah, Moses' wife, after he had sent her back," the bringing (Exodus 4:24, Keil, Gill); "took Zipporah, Moses' wife," the wife (Exodus 2:21, Gill); "after he had sent her back," the separation (vv.5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bringing Jethro. The verse marks the Zipporah: brought. The verse displays the wife: of Moses. The verse displays the sending-back: earlier. The verse displays the reunion: to come. The bringing displays Moses' family restored. The verse displays the Jethro: bringing. The verse displays the Zipporah: restored. The verse displays the family: reunited. Jethro bringing Zipporah, Moses' wife, displays the family restored: Jethro took Zipporah, after she had been sent back (Exodus 4:24-26) — the wife brought to Moses, the family reunited at the mount. The verse displays Moses' sons (vv.3-4). That the wife and sons whom Moses had sent home in the press of the conflict were now restored to him at the mount of God shows the providence which, having required the surrender of the family for the work's sake, gives them back again in its own time, that the deliverer might be comforted in his own house even as he led the people of God (Exodus 4:20; 18:5).
3:
"And her two sons; of which the name of the one was Gershom; for he said, I have been an alien in a strange land:" The very brief verse notes Gershom, the son named for Moses' exile. The grammatical voices note the name. Wesley and Gill note that "her two sons; of which the name of the one was Gershom; for he said, I have been an alien in a strange land" — Jethro brought Moses' two sons; the first, Gershom ("a stranger there"), named because Moses had been an alien (a stranger, sojourner) in a strange land (Midian, Exodus 2:22); the name of the exile; the memorial of Moses' pilgrim state.

On "the name of the one was Gershom," the witnesses note the son. Gill notes Gershom (Moses' firstborn, Exodus 2:22) — the son of the exile; the name recalling Moses' sojourning.

On "I have been an alien in a strange land," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the pilgrim: "I have been an alien in a strange land" — Moses owning himself a stranger (in Midian, the land of his exile); the pilgrim spirit (the godly strangers on earth, Hebrews 11:13-16); the name a testimony to Moses' faith (looking beyond the exile to the promise). The verse displays Gershom, the son named for Moses' exile.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "the name of the one was Gershom... I have been an alien in a strange land," the exile (Exodus 2:22, Wesley, Gill); "the name of the one was Gershom," the son (Gill); "I have been an alien in a strange land," the pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named Gershom. The verse marks the son: Gershom. The verse displays the name: a stranger. The verse displays the exile: Moses'. The verse displays the pilgrim: spirit. The name displays Moses' pilgrim state. The verse displays the Gershom: named. The verse displays the alien: in Midian. The verse displays the pilgrim: hope. Gershom, the son named for Moses' exile, displays the pilgrim state: Gershom, for I have been an alien in a strange land (Exodus 2:22; Hebrews 11:13-16) — the name of the exile, the pilgrim spirit of the godly. The verse displays Eliezer (v.4).
4:
"And the name of the other was Eliezer; for the God of my father, said he, was mine help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh:" The very brief verse notes Eliezer, the son named for God's help. The grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "the name of the other was Eliezer; for the God of my father, said he, was mine help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh" — Moses' second son, Eliezer ("my God is help"), named because the God of his father had been his help and delivered him from Pharaoh's sword (when Pharaoh sought to slay him, Exodus 2:15); the name of thanksgiving (commemorating God's deliverance); the testimony to God's help.

On "the God of my father... was mine help," the reformed and witnesses note the help. Gill notes the God of Moses' father (the covenant God) the help and deliverer — the thanksgiving; the faith owning God's deliverance.

On "delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh" — God's deliverance of Moses (from Pharaoh's wrath, Exodus 2:15); the name a memorial of the rescue; the faith (the godly naming their children for God's mercies, the providence remembered). The verse displays Eliezer, the son named for God's help.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "the name of the other was Eliezer; for the God of my father... was mine help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh," the deliverance (Exodus 2:15, Ellicott, Gill); "the God of my father... was mine help," the help (Gill); "delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh," the deliverance (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named Eliezer. The verse marks the son: Eliezer. The verse displays the help: God's. The verse displays the deliverance: from Pharaoh. The verse displays the thanksgiving: owned. The name displays God's help to Moses. The verse displays the Eliezer: named. The verse displays the God: the help. The verse displays the deliverance: remembered. Eliezer, the son named for God's help, displays the thanksgiving: Eliezer, for the God of my father was mine help, delivered me from Pharaoh's sword (Exodus 2:15) — the name commemorating God's deliverance, the faith owning his mercy. The verse displays Jethro's coming to Moses (vv.5-6).
5:
"And Jethro, Moses' father in law, came with his sons and his wife unto Moses into the wilderness, where he encamped at the mount of God:" The very brief verse notes Jethro coming to Moses at the mount of God. The grammatical voices note the arrival. JFB and Gill note that "Jethro, Moses' father in law, came with his sons and his wife unto Moses into the wilderness, where he encamped at the mount of God" — Jethro came with Moses' wife and sons to Moses in the wilderness, where Israel was encamped at the mount of God (Horeb/Sinai, Exodus 3:1; 19:1-2); the visit (the Gentile priest coming to the camp); the reunion of the family; the place (the mount of God, where the law would soon be given).

On "came with his sons and his wife unto Moses," the witnesses note the family. Gill notes Jethro bringing the family (the wife and sons) — the reunion; the visit.

On "where he encamped at the mount of God," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the place: "where he encamped at the mount of God" — Israel at the mount of God (Horeb/Sinai, the place of the burning bush, Exodus 3:1, and soon of the law, Exodus 19); the providence (the camp at the holy mount); the setting for Jethro's counsel (vv.13-26) before the giving of the law. The verse displays Jethro coming to Moses at the mount of God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the arrival — "Jethro... came with his sons and his wife unto Moses... at the mount of God," the visit (Exodus 3:1, JFB, Gill); "came with his sons and his wife unto Moses," the family (Gill); "where he encamped at the mount of God," the place (Exodus 19:1, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arriving Jethro. The verse marks the visit: to Moses. The verse displays the family: brought. The verse displays the mount: of God. The verse displays the reunion: at the camp. The arrival displays Jethro coming to the camp. The verse displays the Jethro: arriving. The verse displays the family: reunited. The verse displays the mount: holy. Jethro coming to Moses at the mount of God displays the visit and reunion: Jethro came with the family to the mount of God (Exodus 3:1; 19:1-2) — the Gentile priest to the camp, the family reunited at the holy mount. The verse displays the meeting (v.7).
6:
"And he said unto Moses, I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee, and thy wife, and her two sons with her." The very brief verse notes Jethro announcing his coming to Moses. The grammatical voices note the announcement. Gill and Clarke note that "he said unto Moses, I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee, and thy wife, and her two sons with her" — Jethro sent word (or announced) to Moses that he had come, with Moses' wife and two sons; the message (perhaps sent ahead, that Moses might come out to meet them, v.7); the announcement of the visit; the bringing of the family.

On "I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee," the witnesses note the message. Gill notes Jethro's announcement (sending word of his coming) — the courteous notice; the message ahead of the meeting.

On "thy wife, and her two sons with her," the grammatical-historical note the family: "thy wife, and her two sons with her" — the wife and sons brought (the reunion); the family restored to Moses; the providence (the family reunited before the meeting, v.7). The verse displays Jethro announcing his coming to Moses.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the announcement — "I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee, and thy wife, and her two sons," the message (v.7, Gill, Clarke); "I thy father in law Jethro am come unto thee," the notice (Gill); "thy wife, and her two sons with her," the family (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the announcing Jethro. The verse marks the message: I am come. The verse displays the wife: brought. The verse displays the sons: with her. The verse displays the meeting: to come. The announcement displays Jethro's notice of the visit. The verse displays the Jethro: announcing. The verse displays the family: brought. The verse displays the meeting: near. Jethro announcing his coming to Moses displays the notice of the visit: I am come, with thy wife and sons (v.7) — the courteous message, the family restored, before the meeting. The verse displays the meeting and reverence (v.7).
7:
"And Moses went out to meet his father in law, and did obeisance, and kissed him; and they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent." The witnesses note Moses' respectful welcome of Jethro. On "Moses went out to meet his father in law, and did obeisance, and kissed him," the grammatical voices note the welcome. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "Moses went out to meet his father in law, and did obeisance, and kissed him" — Moses, the great leader of Israel, went out to meet Jethro, bowed (did obeisance), and kissed him; the respect and affection (Moses honoring his father-in-law, despite his own high station); the courtesy (the meekness of Moses, honoring the aged and the kindred, Exodus 20:12).

On "did obeisance, and kissed him," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the humility. Calvin and Ellicott note Moses' obeisance and kiss (the respectful, affectionate greeting) — the humility of the great leader; the honor to the father-in-law; the meekness of Moses (Numbers 12:3).

On "they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the fellowship: "they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent" — the friendly inquiry (after each other's welfare) and the going into the tent (the hospitality, the fellowship); the warm reception; the providence (the meeting that would lead to Jethro's worship, v.12, and counsel, vv.13-26). The verse displays Moses' respectful welcome of Jethro.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the welcome — "Moses went out to meet his father in law, and did obeisance, and kissed him... they came into the tent," the respect (Exodus 20:12, Keil, Henry); "did obeisance, and kissed him," the humility (Numbers 12:3, Calvin, Ellicott); "they asked each other of their welfare; and they came into the tent," the fellowship (v.12, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the welcoming Moses. The verse marks the welcome: went out. The verse displays the obeisance: and kiss. The verse displays the inquiry: of welfare. The verse displays the tent: entered. The welcome displays Moses' meekness and honor. The verse displays the Moses: welcoming. The verse displays the Jethro: honored. The verse displays the fellowship: warm. Moses' respectful welcome of Jethro displays the meekness and honor: Moses did obeisance and kissed him (Exodus 20:12; Numbers 12:3) — the great leader humbly honoring his father-in-law, the warm fellowship. The verse displays the rehearsal of God's works (v.8).
8:
"And Moses told his father in law all that the LORD had done unto Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Israel's sake, and all the travail that had come upon them by the way, and how the LORD delivered them." The very brief verse notes Moses recounting God's deliverance to Jethro. The grammatical voices note the rehearsal. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses told his father in law all that the LORD had done unto Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Israel's sake, and all the travail... and how the LORD delivered them" — Moses recounted to Jethro all the LORD had done (the plagues, the Red Sea, the wonders), the hardships (travail) of the way, and the deliverances; the testimony to God's works (Moses ascribing all to the LORD); the rehearsal of the redemption.

On "all that the LORD had done unto Pharaoh and to the Egyptians," the reformed and witnesses note the works. Gill notes Moses recounting God's works (the deliverance, the judgments) — the testimony; the glory given to God.

On "all the travail... and how the LORD delivered them," the grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "all the travail that had come upon them by the way, and how the LORD delivered them" — the hardships (the trials of the wilderness) and the deliverances (God's repeated help); the testimony to God's faithfulness; the providence (the rehearsal that moved Jethro to rejoice and bless God, vv.9-11). The verse displays Moses recounting God's deliverance to Jethro.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rehearsal — "Moses told... all that the LORD had done... and how the LORD delivered them," the testimony (Ellicott, Gill); "all that the LORD had done unto Pharaoh and to the Egyptians," the works (Gill); "all the travail... and how the LORD delivered them," the deliverance (vv.9-11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recounting Moses. The verse marks the rehearsal: God's works. The verse displays the travail: of the way. The verse displays the deliverance: by the LORD. The verse displays the testimony: given. The rehearsal displays Moses ascribing all to God. The verse displays the Moses: recounting. The verse displays the LORD: glorified. The verse displays the Jethro: hearing. Moses recounting God's deliverance to Jethro displays the testimony to God's works: Moses told all the LORD had done, and how he delivered them (vv.9-11) — the glory given to God, moving Jethro to rejoice. The verse displays Jethro's joy (v.9).
9:
"And Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel, whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians." The very brief verse notes Jethro rejoicing in God's goodness to Israel. The grammatical voices note the joy. Gill and Clarke note that "Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel, whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians" — Jethro rejoiced (was glad) at hearing of all God's goodness to Israel (the deliverance, vv.8); the joy of the Gentile priest (rejoicing in God's mercy to his people); the gladness at God's works; the response of faith.

On "rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel," the reformed and witnesses note the gladness. Gill notes Jethro's joy (rejoicing in God's goodness, not envying) — the godly gladness; the sharing in the joy of God's people (Romans 12:15, rejoicing with them that rejoice).

On "whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians," the grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians" — the deliverance (the cause of the joy); Jethro rejoicing in the redemption; the providence (the Gentile priest glad at God's mercy, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles rejoicing in God's salvation, Romans 15:10). The verse displays Jethro rejoicing in God's goodness to Israel.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the joy — "Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel," the gladness (vv.8, Gill, Clarke); "rejoiced for all the goodness which the LORD had done to Israel," the joy (Romans 12:15, Gill); "whom he had delivered out of the hand of the Egyptians," the deliverance (Romans 15:10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rejoicing Jethro. The verse marks the joy: rejoiced. The verse displays the goodness: of the LORD. The verse displays the deliverance: from Egypt. The verse displays the gladness: of faith. The joy displays Jethro glad at God's mercy. The verse displays the Jethro: rejoicing. The verse displays the LORD: praised. The verse displays the Gentile: glad. Jethro rejoicing in God's goodness to Israel displays the gladness of faith: Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness the LORD had done (Romans 12:15; 15:10) — the Gentile priest glad at God's mercy, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles' joy in salvation. The verse displays Jethro's confession (vv.10-11).
10:
"And Jethro said, Blessed be the LORD, who hath delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of Pharaoh, who hath delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians." The very brief verse notes Jethro blessing the LORD for the deliverance. The grammatical voices note the blessing. Calvin and Gill note that "Jethro said, Blessed be the LORD, who hath delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of Pharaoh" — Jethro blessed (praised) the LORD for delivering Israel from the Egyptians and Pharaoh; the worship of the Gentile priest (ascribing the glory to the LORD); the blessing of God for the redemption; the response of faith.

On "Blessed be the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the praise. Calvin and Gill note Jethro's blessing of the LORD (the doxology, the praise of God for the deliverance) — the worship; the glory given to God.

On "who hath delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians," the grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "who hath delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians" — the deliverance praised (the redemption of Israel); the providence (the Gentile priest blessing God, a foreshadowing of the nations praising God for his salvation, Psalm 117:1; Romans 15:11). The verse displays Jethro blessing the LORD for the deliverance.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "Blessed be the LORD, who hath delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians... from under the hand of the Egyptians," the praise (Calvin, Gill); "Blessed be the LORD," the doxology (Calvin, Gill); "who hath delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians," the deliverance (Psalm 117:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessing Jethro. The verse marks the blessing: blessed be the LORD. The verse displays the deliverance: from Egypt. The verse displays the praise: of God. The verse displays the worship: of the Gentile. The blessing displays Jethro praising God for the redemption. The verse displays the Jethro: blessing. The verse displays the LORD: praised. The verse displays the deliverance: glorified. Jethro blessing the LORD for the deliverance displays the praise of God: blessed be the LORD, who hath delivered the people (Psalm 117:1; Romans 15:11) — the Gentile priest praising God, a foreshadowing of the nations' praise. The verse displays Jethro's confession (v.11).
11:
"Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods: for in the thing wherein they dealt proudly he was above them." The witnesses note Jethro's confession that the LORD is greater than all gods. On "Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods," interpreters expound the confession. The reformed and the dominant reading note Jethro's words — "Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods": Jethro confessed, on hearing of the deliverance, that the LORD (Jehovah) is greater than all gods (the gods of Egypt, of the nations); the confession of the Gentile priest (owning the supremacy of the true God, perhaps a fuller conversion, or a confirmation of faith); the witnesses note the confession — the LORD above all the gods (the false gods of Egypt judged, Exodus 12:12; the incomparable God, Exodus 15:11, "who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods?"); the knowledge of God by his works (Jethro learning, by the deliverance, the supremacy of the LORD); a foreshadowing of the Gentiles confessing the true God (the nations coming to the light, Isaiah 60:3; the firstfruits of the Gentile confession).

On "for in the thing wherein they dealt proudly he was above them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the judgment. Wesley and JFB note the ground of the confession — "in the thing wherein they dealt proudly he was above them": in the very matter where the Egyptians dealt proudly (the oppression, the drowning of the infants, the pursuit), God was above them (overruling, judging, the proud drowned in the sea they thought to use, Exodus 14:27-28); the just retribution (God paying the proud in their own coin, the Egyptians drowned as they drowned the infants); the proof of God's supremacy (the proud humbled, the LORD exalted).

On the confession, the puritan and grammatical-historical note the knowledge: Jethro's "now I know" — the knowledge of God's supremacy by the deliverance; the confession of faith; the providence (the works of God teaching the knowledge of the LORD, even to the Gentile). The verse displays Jethro's confession that the LORD is greater than all gods.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jethro confessed the LORD greater than all gods, proven by his judgment on the proud Egyptians (Wesley, JFB, Gill). The verse bears its weight as Jethro's confession: "the LORD is greater than all gods" — the Gentile priest owning the supremacy of the true God, learned by the deliverance (Exodus 12:12; 15:11), the false gods of Egypt judged; the just retribution where the proud were humbled in their own pride (Exodus 14:27-28); a foreshadowing of the Gentiles confessing the true God (Isaiah 60:3). The verse displays the confession crux. The verse marks the confession: the LORD greater. The verse displays the gods: surpassed. The verse displays the pride: judged. The verse displays the knowledge: by the works. The confession displays Jethro owning the supremacy of the LORD. The verse displays the Jethro: confessing. The verse displays the LORD: supreme. The verse displays the Gentile: believing. Jethro's confession that the LORD is greater than all gods displays the knowledge of God's supremacy: now I know the LORD is greater than all gods (Exodus 12:12; 15:11; Isaiah 60:3) — the Gentile priest owning the true God, the proud Egyptians judged, a foreshadowing of the Gentiles' confession. The verse displays Jethro's sacrifice (v.12).
12:
"And Jethro, Moses' father in law, took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God: and Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God." The witnesses note Jethro's sacrifice and the fellowship meal. On "Jethro... took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God," the grammatical voices note the sacrifice. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jethro... took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God" — Jethro offered a burnt offering and sacrifices to God (an act of worship and thanksgiving for the deliverance); the worship of the Gentile priest (offering to the true God); the sacrifice of praise; the confession in act (the worship following the confession, v.11).

On "took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the worship. Calvin and Wesley note Jethro's offering (the burnt offering, the sacrifices) — the worship of God; the thanksgiving for the deliverance; the priest of Midian offering to Jehovah.

On "Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the fellowship: "Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God" — the fellowship meal (Aaron and the elders eating with Jethro, the sacrificial feast, before God); the communion (the people joined in worship and fellowship with the Gentile priest); the providence (the fellowship a foreshadowing of the Gentiles joined to Israel in the worship of God, the one table, Ephesians 2:11-19; a type of the gospel feast, Jew and Gentile together). The verse displays Jethro's sacrifice and the fellowship meal.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sacrifice — "Jethro... took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God: and Aaron came, and all the elders... to eat bread... before God," the worship and fellowship (v.11, Ellicott, Gill); "took a burnt offering and sacrifices for God," the worship (Calvin, Wesley); "Aaron came, and all the elders of Israel, to eat bread with Moses' father in law before God," the fellowship (Ephesians 2:11, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshipping Jethro. The verse marks the offering: burnt offering. The verse displays the worship: of God. The verse displays the elders: at the meal. The verse displays the fellowship: before God. The sacrifice displays Jethro worshipping the true God. The verse displays the Jethro: offering. The verse displays the elders: feasting. The verse displays the fellowship: with the Gentile. Jethro's sacrifice and the fellowship meal display the worship and communion: Jethro offered sacrifices, and the elders ate bread before God (Ephesians 2:11-19) — the Gentile priest worshipping, the fellowship of Jew and Gentile, a foreshadowing of the gospel feast. The verse displays the people's cause coming to Moses (v.13).
13:
"And it came to pass on the morrow, that Moses sat to judge the people: and the people stood by Moses from the morning unto the evening." The witnesses note Moses judging the people alone all day. On "Moses sat to judge the people," the grammatical voices note the office. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "Moses sat to judge the people: and the people stood by Moses from the morning unto the evening" — Moses sat as judge over the people, hearing their causes and disputes; the people standing before him from morning to evening (the great burden of the cases); the sole judge (Moses alone bearing the whole weight, vv.14); the toil of the office.

On "the people stood by Moses from the morning unto the evening," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the burden. Calvin and Ellicott note the great burden (the people waiting all day, the cases endless) — the overload (Moses alone unable to bear it, v.18); the toil of the sole judge.

21:
"Moreover thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness; and place such over them, to be rulers of thousands, and rulers of hundreds, rulers of fifties, and rulers of tens:" The witnesses note the qualifications of the rulers — the crux of the godly judge. On "thou shalt provide out of all the people able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness," interpreters expound the qualifications. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the four marks — "able men, such as fear God, men of truth, hating covetousness": Jethro counseled Moses to choose, for rulers and judges, men marked by four qualifications: (1) "able men" (men of ability, capacity, courage, wisdom, fit for the office); (2) "such as fear God" (the fear of God the root of righteous judgment, men who reverence God and so will judge justly, 2 Samuel 23:3, "he that ruleth over men must be just, ruling in the fear of God"); (3) "men of truth" (honest, faithful, truthful men, not liars or flatterers); (4) "hating covetousness" (hating bribes and unjust gain, not to be bought, Deuteronomy 16:19, "a gift doth blind the eyes of the wise"); the witnesses note these as the abiding qualifications of all who rule and judge (in church and state) — ability, piety, integrity, and freedom from greed; the model for the choice of rulers, elders, and officers (the like qualifications, Deuteronomy 1:13; Acts 6:3, "men of honest report, full of the Holy Ghost and wisdom"; 1 Timothy 3:1-7; Titus 1:5-9, the elder not greedy of filthy lucre); the standard for godly government.

On "place such over them, to be rulers of thousands... and rulers of tens," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the order. The witnesses agree: the appointment — "Moses chose able men... and made them heads... rulers of thousands... and rulers of tens," the plan carried out (v.21, Keil, Gill); "Moses chose able men out of all Israel," the choice (Deuteronomy 1:13, Gill); "rulers of thousands... and rulers of tens," the order (vv.21-22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appointing Moses. The verse marks the choice: able men. The verse displays the rulers: appointed. The verse displays the order: graded. The verse displays the government: ordered. The appointment displays the counsel carried out. The verse displays the Moses: appointing. The verse displays the judges: set. The verse displays the burden: shared. Moses appointing the rulers as counseled displays the plan carried out: Moses chose able men, rulers of thousands and tens (v.21; Deuteronomy 1:13) — the judges appointed, the burden shared, the government ordered. The verse displays the judging of the people (v.26).
26:
"And they judged the people at all seasons: the hard causes they brought unto Moses, but every small matter they judged themselves." The very brief verse notes the judges judging while Moses kept the hard cases. The grammatical voices note the order. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they judged the people at all seasons: the hard causes they brought unto Moses, but every small matter they judged themselves" — the appointed judges judged the people at all times, bringing the hard cases to Moses and deciding the small matters themselves; the order working (the plan in effect, vv.22); the shared burden (the lesser cases to the judges, the harder to Moses); the relief of Moses.

On "the hard causes they brought unto Moses," the witnesses note the order. Gill notes the hard cases to Moses (the great matters reserved for the chief, vv.22) — the graded jurisdiction; the order maintained.

On "every small matter they judged themselves," the grammatical-historical note the relief: "every small matter they judged themselves" — the small cases decided by the judges (the burden shared); the relief of Moses (the load divided, vv.22); the providence (the orderly government, the wisdom of the plan working). The verse displays the judges judging while Moses kept the hard cases.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the order — "they judged the people... the hard causes they brought unto Moses, but every small matter they judged themselves," the plan working (v.22, Keil, Gill); "the hard causes they brought unto Moses," the order (Gill); "every small matter they judged themselves," the relief (vv.22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the judging judges. The verse marks the judging: at all seasons. The verse displays the hard cases: to Moses. The verse displays the small: judged themselves. The verse displays the burden: shared. The order displays the plan working. The verse displays the judges: judging. The verse displays the Moses: the hard cases. The verse displays the relief: gained. The judges judging while Moses kept the hard cases display the plan working: the hard causes to Moses, the small matters they judged (v.22) — the burden shared, the relief of Moses, the orderly government. The verse displays Jethro's departure (v.27).
27:
"And Moses let his father in law depart; and he went his way into his own land." The very brief verse notes Jethro departing to his own land. The grammatical voices note the departure. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses let his father in law depart; and he went his way into his own land" — Moses sent Jethro away (let him depart), and Jethro returned to his own land (Midian); the parting (after the visit, the worship, the counsel); the return of the Gentile priest (Numbers 10:29-32, the later invitation; the Kenites, his descendants, dwelling with Israel, Judges 1:16); the close of the visit.

On "Moses let his father in law depart," the witnesses note the parting. Gill notes Moses sending Jethro away (with honor, after the visit) — the parting; the close of the fellowship.

On "he went his way into his own land," the grammatical-historical note the return: "he went his way into his own land" — Jethro returning to Midian (his own land); the providence (the Gentile priest who had worshipped and counseled now departing; the Kenites later joined to Israel, Judges 1:16; 1 Samuel 15:6, the kindness to the descendants of Jethro); the close of the episode before the giving of the law (ch. 19). The verse displays Jethro departing to his own land.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the departure — "Moses let his father in law depart; and he went his way into his own land," the parting (Numbers 10:29, Ellicott, Gill); "Moses let his father in law depart," the parting (Gill); "he went his way into his own land," the return (Judges 1:16, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the departing Jethro. The verse marks the departure: let him go. The verse displays the return: to his land. The verse displays the parting: honorable. The verse displays the visit: closed. The departure displays Jethro returning home. The verse displays the Jethro: departing. The verse displays the land: Midian. The verse displays the visit: ended. Jethro departing to his own land displays the close of the visit: Moses let Jethro depart to his own land (Numbers 10:29-32; Judges 1:16) — the Gentile priest returning home, the visit ended before the giving of the law. The verse closes the chapter, before Sinai (ch.19).
